vulnerabilities.description: Potential Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) in Workday fetcher - host, tenant, and site parameters from configuration are used directly in URL construction without validation. An attacker with write access to sources.yaml could set host to internal IPs (127.0.0.1, 169.254.169.254) to access internal services
Missing port validation in SMTP configuration - SMTP_PORT is cast directly to int without bounds checking. Invalid ports (0, >65535) could cause runtime errors
Potential ReDoS vulnerability - regex patterns using .* on untrusted input could cause catastrophic backtracking if API returns malformed HTML
Unhandled SMTP authentication exceptions - could leak information about invalid credentials or missing environment variables in error messages

strengths: SQL injection protected - all database queries use parameterized statements
No hardcoded secrets - all credentials loaded from environment variables/GitHub secrets
Proper HTTPS for all external requests using httpx
YAML configuration uses safe_load() preventing code injection
Input filtering properly escapes regex patterns using re.escape()
Appropriate use of timeouts and retry logic with exponential backoff
No deserialization of untrusted data (pickle, eval, exec)
recommendations: Validate Workday configuration parameters: add URL scheme validation and hostname allowlist to prevent SSRF
Add SMTP port bounds checking: if port < 1 or port > 65535, raise ValueError before connecting
Wrap SMTP login in try-except to handle AuthenticationError gracefully without exposing credentials
Add rate limiting headers and respect Retry-After from HTTP responses to prevent API abuse
Consider pinning certificate validation for Twilio and other external API calls if possible
Add pre-commit hook using detect-secrets to prevent future credential commits
Use urllib.parse.urlparse() to validate URLs from API responses before fetching
Add timeout to all subprocess calls in agent.py (Chrome rendering)
Document security model: clarify that sources.yaml must be trusted (version-controlled, not user-input)
